text.title: growth-brand-positioning Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for growth-brand-positioning. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Be ruthlessly specific about the target customer: name role, company type and stage, goal, and obstacle. "Businesses" or "developers" is not a target customer.
rule.rationale: Positioning that resonates with everyone resonates with no one. A specific target customer description disqualifies wrong-fit prospects by design, which is a feature not a bug — it raises conversion among fit buyers.

rule.statement: List at least 3 competitive alternatives including: direct competitors, indirect competitors, the status quo (doing nothing), and substitutes (DIY, hire someone). Frame positioning against this list explicitly.
rule.rationale: Customers always frame their decision against alternatives. Owning the framing prevents competitors from defining it. Without alternatives, positioning cannot be differentiated — "X is the Y" has no meaning without "unlike Z".

rule.statement: Identify the one thing only your product can claim: a unique feature, approach, focus, expertise level, experience quality, proven result metric, or ecosystem integration. Pick one; more than one dilutes the claim.
rule.rationale: Multi-pillar positioning is forgettable. Single-pillar positioning compounds over 12+ months into recognition. Competitors can copy features; they cannot easily copy a proven result tied to a specific methodology or a deep ecosystem integration.

rule.statement: Choose one of four category strategies: existing category (easiest to understand), sub-category (differentiate in crowded market), new category (create a space), or versus (challenger positioning). Only create a new category if you can fund market education for 2+ years.
rule.rationale: Category creation looks attractive but creates an extra job: educating the market about a problem space before selling the solution. Most early-stage products should use sub-category framing to inherit existing search demand while carving a distinct niche.

rule.statement: Write the internal positioning statement in this form: "For [target customer] who [situation/need], [Product] is the [category] that [key benefit], unlike [alternatives] we [unique differentiator]."
rule.rationale: This structure forces explicit answers to all five positioning questions simultaneously and surfaces gaps (vague differentiator, undefined category) that prose descriptions hide.

rule.statement: Test positioning by asking customers to describe you to a peer. If they invent their own version, the positioning is not sticking. Test with 5+ customers before treating it as stable.
rule.rationale: Internal positioning that sounds sharp often dissolves in the field. Customer language is the ground truth for whether positioning actually transferred.
